title: Working in Public
author: Nadia Eghbal
summary: A field guide to how modern open-source work actually runs once attention, maintainer labor, and online communities become the real constraints.
whyRead: Useful if you are building public catalogs, skills, or open-source proof surfaces and want a clearer mental model for why some projects feel alive while others become unpaid support desks.
notes: Read it less as an open-source book and more as a book about attention markets.
Pairs naturally with any radar project because it explains why curation and contribution rules matter.
nextStep: Read chapter 3, then write one contribution rule your project should enforce.
markdown:
# Working in Public

Author: Nadia Eghbal
Shelf: Internet & Open Source

Why George recommends it:
Working in Public explains the hidden labor behind open-source projects: attention,
community, maintainership, contribution queues, and the weird economics of public
software. It is especially useful if you are building public proof systems or
catalogs that invite other people in.

Best for:
- Understanding maintainers as community operators
- Thinking about contribution funnels
- Designing lighter public project surfaces

Next step:
Read chapter 3, then write one contribution rule your project should enforce.

title: The Beginning of Infinity
author: David Deutsch
summary: A big, opinionated argument that progress comes from better explanations and that good explanations unlock more growth than most people expect.
whyRead: Good when you want a more ambitious frame for learning, building, and why compounding knowledge matters.
notes: This is not a quick productivity book. It is a worldview book.
The useful move is to ask which of your current beliefs are bad explanations pretending to be constraints.
nextStep: Read one chapter slowly and write the strongest explanation it changes.
markdown:
# The Beginning of Infinity

Author: David Deutsch
Shelf: Models

Why George recommends it:
The book argues that progress is not a finite pile of discoveries. It compounds
when people create better explanations. That makes it a useful read for anyone
trying to build systems, taste, or a durable worldview.

Best for:
- Long-range optimism without self-help fluff
- Thinking about explanations as leverage
- Resetting from tactics to worldview

Next step:
Read one chapter slowly and write the strongest explanation it changes.

title: The Effective Engineer
author: Edmond Lau
summary: A practical book about leverage, feedback loops, iteration speed, and choosing engineering work that actually matters.
whyRead: Useful for builders who are drowning in implementation details and need a sharper lens for picking the next useful action.
notes: The core question is not whether you are busy. It is whether your work changes future work.
This pairs well with weekly planning or sprint retros.
nextStep: Pick one repeated task this week and automate or delete the worst part.
markdown:
# The Effective Engineer

Author: Edmond Lau
Shelf: Engineering

Why George recommends it:
The Effective Engineer is a leverage book disguised as an engineering career
book. It is good when your week is full of plausible work and you need to choose
which work compounds.

Best for:
- Prioritizing engineering work
- Building leverage through tooling
- Improving feedback loops

Next step:
Pick one repeated task this week and automate or delete the worst part.

title: A Pattern Language
author: Christopher Alexander, Sara Ishikawa, Murray Silverstein
summary: A catalog of design patterns for towns, buildings, rooms, and daily life, useful far outside architecture.
whyRead: A reminder that great design often comes from reusable patterns with human reasons underneath them, not from arbitrary visual taste.
bestFor: Designing spaces and interfaces around behavior
notes: Do not try to read it straight through. Browse it like a catalog.
The format itself is the lesson: named pattern, context, tradeoff, prescription.
nextStep: Pick three patterns and translate them into software or workspace rules.
markdown:
# A Pattern Language

Author: Christopher Alexander, Sara Ishikawa, Murray Silverstein
Shelf: Design

Why George recommends it:
This is one of the best examples of a durable catalog format. Each pattern names
a recurring human problem and gives it a reusable shape. That makes it useful
for product design, writing, tools, and personal systems.

Best for:
- Thinking in reusable patterns
- Designing spaces and interfaces around behavior
- Naming recurring design instincts

Next step:
Pick three patterns and translate them into software or workspace rules.

title: The Dream Machine
author: M. Mitchell Waldrop
summary: A history of J.C.R. Licklider and the research culture that led toward personal computing, networks, and interactive machines.
whyRead: Good for remembering that software progress is often a taste-and-institution story, not only a technical invention story.
bestFor: Understanding the lineage of interactive computing
Getting unstuck from narrow product thinking
notes: The lasting lesson is how much computing was shaped by people who could imagine a different relationship with machines.
Useful context for agent work because it widens the frame beyond today's tooling names.
nextStep: Read the Licklider sections and write what today's agent tooling still lacks.
markdown:
# The Dream Machine

Author: M. Mitchell Waldrop
Shelf: Computing History

Why George recommends it:
The Dream Machine is a history of interactive computing as a worldview. It
shows how much of modern software came from people who could imagine a better
relationship between humans and computers before the hardware was ready.

Best for:
- Understanding the lineage of interactive computing
- Thinking about research taste
- Getting unstuck from narrow product thinking

Next step:
Read the Licklider sections and write what today's agent tooling still lacks.

title: Poor Charlie's Almanack
author: Charlie Munger
summary: A dense collection of Munger's talks and mental models about incentives, psychology, compounding, and practical judgment.
whyRead: Useful when you want to improve decisions without turning everything into a spreadsheet.
notes: The best parts are the repeated warnings about incentives and psychological misjudgment.
Use it as a reference, not a book you need to finish in order.
nextStep: Pick one model and apply it to a current project decision.
markdown:
# Poor Charlie's Almanack

Author: Charlie Munger
Shelf: Judgment

Why George recommends it:
Poor Charlie's Almanack is a mental-model reference for judgment, incentives,
psychology, and compounding. It is useful when you need better defaults for
decisions rather than more productivity tactics.

Best for:
- Mental models
- Decision hygiene
- Spotting incentive problems

Next step:
Pick one model and apply it to a current project decision.

title: The Art of Doing Science and Engineering
author: Richard Hamming
summary: Hamming's lectures on doing important work, building taste, choosing problems, and developing a long-term technical life.
whyRead: A direct antidote to tiny-task drift. It asks whether your effort is pointed at work that could matter.
notes: The important-work question is uncomfortable because it is supposed to be.
Best read in small chunks with notes, not consumed as inspiration.
nextStep: Write the three most important problems in your current work area.
markdown:
# The Art of Doing Science and Engineering

Author: Richard Hamming
Shelf: Taste & Work

Why George recommends it:
Hamming is useful because he does not let technical people hide behind busyness.
The book keeps returning to taste, problem selection, and whether your work has
a chance of mattering.

Best for:
- Choosing better problems
- Technical taste
- Long-term career direction

Next step:
Write the three most important problems in your current work area.

title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
author: Martin Kleppmann
summary: A clear map of databases, distributed systems, streams, consistency, and the tradeoffs behind modern data infrastructure.
whyRead: Worth reading when system complexity stops being theoretical and starts showing up as product behavior.
notes: The value is not memorizing terms. It is getting precise about failure modes.
Keep it nearby when evaluating backend architecture or vendor claims.
nextStep: Read one chapter tied to a real system you maintain or use.
markdown:
# Designing Data-Intensive Applications

Author: Martin Kleppmann
Shelf: Engineering

Why George recommends it:
DDIA gives you language for the invisible tradeoffs behind databases,
distributed systems, streams, consistency, and reliability. It is a serious
engineering reference that pays off when systems get real.

Best for:
- Backend architecture
- Distributed systems tradeoffs
- Understanding reliability language

Next step:
Read one chapter tied to a real system you maintain or use.

title: The Years of Lyndon Johnson
author: Robert A. Caro
summary: A monumental biography series about ambition, institutions, power, and how politics actually works up close.
whyRead: Useful if you want to understand power as a practical operating system rather than a civics-class abstraction.
notes: This is a project, not a casual recommendation.
Start with The Path to Power if you want origins, or Master of the Senate if you want legislative machinery.
nextStep: Read one Caro chapter and map the incentives of every major actor.
markdown:
# The Years of Lyndon Johnson

Author: Robert A. Caro
Shelf: Power

Why George recommends it:
Caro's LBJ books are about ambition, institutions, power, and how systems bend
under pressure. They are useful far beyond politics because they show power as
daily mechanics.

Best for:
- Institutional power
- Biography as systems study
- Longform nonfiction craft

Next step:
Read one Caro chapter and map the incentives of every major actor.

title: The Making of the Atomic Bomb
author: Richard Rhodes
summary: A sweeping history of physics, war, institutions, and the people who turned scientific discovery into world-changing technology.
whyRead: Good when you want the full stack: discovery, personality, institution, engineering, and consequence.
notes: The book is long because the context matters.
It is a reminder that technical breakthroughs are also human and institutional events.
nextStep: Track one discovery from idea to institution to artifact.
markdown:
# The Making of the Atomic Bomb

Author: Richard Rhodes
Shelf: Science History

Why George recommends it:
This book follows discovery, personality, institution-building, engineering, and
consequence in one huge narrative. It is useful when you want to see technology
as more than invention.

Best for:
- Science history
- Understanding institutions under pressure
- Longform narrative nonfiction

Next step:
Track one discovery from idea to institution to artifact.
